text: The surgeon begins at the top of the pelvis, identifying the ureters and dissecting them from surrounding tissue on both sides. The dissection progresses towards the center to address rectal or posterior cervical disease.

The surgeon identifies the left ovary, visible as a shiny white structure on the right side of the screen, adherent to the posterior aspect of the uterus. The bowel is being medialized to clear the surgical field. An epiploic appendix with disease was previously removed. Various colors of endometriosis tissue are noted, including red, white, and black.

The surgeon identifies brownish stained areas within the pelvic cavity, which are visually distinct from typical endometriosis lesions. These areas are noted to contain old blood and macrophages. The surgeon then medializes the rectum, adjusting its position within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses fluorescence to identify the ureter, facilitating anatomical orientation. A cyst is drained to prevent leakage of chocolate-colored fluid, causing the ovary to elevate slightly. As the cyst deflates, the rectum is visibly pulled up towards the posterior aspect of the uterus.
The surgeon gently elevates the ovary and performs a temporary ovarian suspension using a V-lock suture, allowing the assistant to focus on suctioning and other tasks rather than holding the ovary.

The surgeon accidentally cuts the V-lock suture, ties a knot to secure it, and proceeds with dissection along the left pelvic sidewall. The ovary is elevated, and the surgeon uses the right hand for traction and the left hand with scissors for lateral dissection. Fluorescence aids in identifying the ureter, allowing precise dissection along the peritoneum and pelvic sidewall towards the ovarian fossa.

The surgeon creates space on both sides to enter the rectovaginal space, aiming to dissect the sigmoid colon from the posterior aspect of the uterus. A visible nodule with endometriosis is identified on the rectum, and the surgeon prepares to excise the affected tissue.

The surgeon switches instruments and opens the peritoneum to access the retroperitoneal space, beginning dissection at the level of the utero-ovarian vessels. The spaces on both the left and right are opened, revealing the rectum adhered to the posterior aspect of the uterus.

The surgeon carefully identifies and dissects the avascular space between the rectum and the posterior aspect of the uterus. Using a monopolar instrument in short bursts to minimize thermal damage, the surgeon removes the peritoneum affected by endometriosis, ensuring the rectum falls into its normal position. The dissection is performed close to the rectal serosa, with care taken to leave minimal posterior uterine tissue on the rectum for later excision.

The surgeon uses a VALCHA uterine retractor to expose the cervix, revealing the back of the uterus, which is now clean of disease. The surgeon performs additional excision of small areas of remaining endometriosis, restoring normal anatomy. The rectum is observed to have returned to its natural position after the excision of a large area of peritoneum.

The surgeon pulls on the rectum where the nodule is located, freeing fibrotic endometriosis and expanding the rectovaginal space. The lesion is lifted and excised, removing significant disease from the serosal and muscularis layers of the rectum without penetrating the mucosa.

The surgeon uses interrupted sutures of tuovicral to protect the excised area on the rectum. Brown areas on the serosa, which can be rubbed off, are visible and identified as hemocedrine-laden macrophages rather than endometriosis. The appendix also shows superficial disease, which is removed.
